summary: Vendor case study dressed as a tech blog post. Modal publishing a success story about their own paying customer is marketing, not journalism -- every metric is cherry-picked to sell sandboxes. The underlying tech (Poe + sandboxing LLM code) is real and verifiable, which keeps this from sliding into High BS.
career_angle: Sandboxed code execution for LLM outputs is a genuine infrastructure gap -- learn container/microVM isolation (Firecracker, gVisor) if you want to be useful in the AI infra hiring wave. Skip the Modal hype, learn the primitives.

dimensions.rationale: Modal's own blog -- vendor marketing channel, not independent reporting or peer review
Case study format means curated metrics only; no failure modes, cost comparisons, or independent benchmarks shared
Poe is a live product, Modal sandboxes are a real API -- you can verify the technical claims yourself
Maximum conflict: vendor publishing glowing review of own product via customer testimonial they control
Sandboxing LLM-generated code is an accepted best practice; no controversial claims here
'Thousands of sandboxes simultaneously' is vague -- no latency numbers, cost per sandbox, failure rates, or comparison to alternatives
Quora is a real company with a real product (Poe has millions of users); Modal is VC-backed with legit infra credentials
